FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, baseline 2016-2018, 9-10 years old, n=9,208, 2-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time modalities: total screen time, TV/movies, YouTube videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses with OCD as outcome; screen time is independent variable with reported inferential statistics

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports adjusted odds ratios with 95% CIs: total screen time aOR 1.05 (95% CI 1.01-1.09); video games aOR 1.15 (95% CI 1.03-1.28); watching videos aOR 1.11 (95% CI 1.01-1.23)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis examining baseline screen time to 2-year follow-up OCD diagnosis

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2023

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable (exposure), not dependent variable. OCD is the outcome (non-SM variable). This does not meet FT-EC7 criteria which requires SM/phone as dependent variable with only demographic predictors.
notes: Clear inclusion. Prospective design with smartphone/SM exposure and psychiatric outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, Year 2 (2018-2020), participants aged 10-14, n=10,280

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific bedtime screen behaviors: TV/streaming movies/videos, video games, music, phone/texting, social media, chat rooms, Internet browsing, computer/studying; also device in bedroom and phone ringer status

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses examining associations between screen behaviors and sleep outcomes; reports multiple inferential tests with adjusted risk ratios

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports adjusted risk ratios with 95% CIs: TV in bedroom ARR 1.27 (95% CI 1.12-1.44); watching movies/videos ARR 1.15 (95% CI 1.09-1.21); social media ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.10-1.23); video games ARR 1.22 (95% CI 1.14-1.29)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of bedtime screen behaviors and sleep disturbance outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Sleep Health: Journal of the National Sleep Foundation, 2023

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional analysis with sleep as outcome (non-SM variable). FT-EC7 only applies to longitudinal-only studies predicting SM from demographics.
notes: Clear inclusion. Cross-sectional design with multiple SM/phone variables and sleep disturbance outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, baseline 2016-2018 (9-11 years), 1-year follow-up 2017-2019 (10-11 years), n=11,875

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time modalities: TV/movies, videos, video games, texting, video chat, and social media with separate coefficients reported for each modality

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses examining prospective associations between baseline screen time and conduct/oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports prevalence ratios with 95% CIs: social media PR 1.62 (95% CI 1.39-1.87) for conduct disorder; video chat PR 1.21 (95% CI 1.06-1.37) for oppositional defiant disorder; video games PR 1.14 (95% CI 1.07-1.21); total screen time PR 1.05 (95% CI 1.03-1.08)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis examining baseline screen time modalities predicting disruptive behavior disorders at 1-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 2023

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable (exposure), conduct and oppositional defiant disorder are outcomes (behavioral/psychiatric, non-SM variables). Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria.
notes: Clear inclusion. Prospective design with contemporary SM modalities and disruptive behavior disorder outcomes.
